steps.text: Conduct dust sample testing to determine the percentage of combustible dust present.
Measure the bulk density of the dust.
Adjust the layer depth criterion for housekeeping in the NFPA standards using the bulk density and measured volume of dust.

constraints.text: Fugitive emissions from green material processes or equipment can settle on elevated surfaces and dry out (to less than 25 percent moisture), presenting fire and deflagration hazards.

steps.text: Review NFPA 652 (Standard on the Fundamentals of Combustible Dust).
Look into other combustible dust standards and recommended practices.

preconditions.text: The facility or operation must manufacture, process, blend, convey, repackage, generate, or handle combustible dusts or combustible particulate solids, and not fall under the excluded categories (such as consumer quantities, retail facilities, mercantile occupancies, warehousing of sealed containers, or farm buildings).

steps.text: Refer to NFPA 101.
Request information from the employer regarding the electrically classified locations at the facility.
Review the provided electrical classification information.
Inspect the classified locations in the facility to ensure they are properly classified and boundary distances are adequately defined.
Refer to Appendix Q for added guidance if combustible dust fire or explosion hazards are discovered in areas not classified.
Ensure that electrical equipment located in identified classified areas is intrinsically safe, approved, or safe for the locations.
Review equipment labels and manuals, and/or contact the manufacturer of the electrical equipment to determine compliance.

preconditions.text: Prior to proceeding with the combustible dust electrical equipment inspection

constraints.text: Hazardous (classified) locations must be properly documented in accordance with 29 CFR 1910.307(b).
Electrical equipment installed in these locations must be intrinsically safe, approved, or safe for the locations as required by 29 CFR 1910.307(c).

steps.text: Revise policies and procedures for new material safety reviews.
Develop and implement policies and procedures for safety reviews of engineering projects.
Identify West manufacturing facilities that use combustible dusts.
Ensure that West manufacturing facilities incorporate applicable safety precautions described in NFPA 654.
Improve hazard communication programs so that the hazards of combustible dust are clearly identified and communicated to the workforce.

constraints.text: Ensure that penetrations of partitions, floors, walls, and ceilings are sealed dust-tight.
Ensure that spaces inaccessible to housekeeping are sealed to prevent dust accumulation.
Ensure that the most current MSDSs are in use and that employees receive training on the revised/updated information.

steps.text: Develop procedures to maintain safety during non-routine operating conditions, such as the loss of oven temperature control.
Revise the incident investigation program to ensure that the underlying causes of incidents such as fires are identified and corrective actions implemented.
Develop a combustible dust safety program using good practice guidelines, such as NFPA 654.
Minimize surfaces where combustible dust could accumulate in the design or modification of the plant.
Ensure phenolic resin-handling facilities are designed to prevent the spread of fires or explosions involving combustible dust.
Prevent the unsafe accumulation and dispersion of combustible dust by frequently cleaning process areas, including locations above production lines.
Minimize the dispersion of combustible dust by using appropriate dust-cleaning methods and tools.
Address the dangers of combustible dust and the prevention of dust explosions in the hazard communication training program.

constraints.text: Loss of oven temperature control can lead to unsafe non-routine operating conditions.
Dust accumulations can disperse into the air and ignite, causing a devastating cascade of fires and explosions.

steps.text: Verify receipt of required training to perform a combustible dust inspection.
Review the current NEP.
Verify possession of required PPE and sampling supplies.
Consider the hazards and characteristics of the combustible dust(s) expected at the site.
Determine and be familiar with the applicable OSHA standards.
Review Appendix C, Common Inspection Questions section of this chapter.

preconditions.text: Must have received the required training to perform a combustible dust inspection. If not trained, contact supervisor.
Must be familiar with the requirements of the NEP before performing a combustible dust inspection.
Must obtain the PPE and sampling supplies before proceeding.

constraints.text: PPE must include a hard hat, safety glasses, and FRC.
Supplies must include 1-liter sample bottles, an aluminum scoop, a natural bristle brush, a measuring tool, pen and paper, and a camera.
Additional PPE and sampling supplies are necessary when low-MIE combustible dusts are present.

steps.text: Ask managers and employees for a copy of the incident reports regarding previous fires or explosions.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the OSHA 300 forms and/or other reports regarding injuries or fatalities caused by fires or explosions.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the written procedures and work practices in place to prevent or mitigate fires, deflagrations, and explosions from combustible dusts.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the written housekeeping procedure(s) with regular cleaning frequencies and methods established.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the documents showing that housekeeping in the facility is routinely inspected and documented.

constraints.text: OSHA standards require control of combustible dust hazards posed by excessive accumulations of combustible dust.

steps.text: Ask managers, engineers, and maintenance workers for a copy of maintenance records to verify if combustible dust-handling and dust-related equipment and systems are inspected, tested, and maintained in accordance with industry standards.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the ignition control program.
Ask managers and employees if personnel who perform manual emptying or filling of containers or vessels are grounded during these operations.
Verify if combustible dust handling equipment and systems are constructed of non-combustible materials.

preconditions.text: Dust has a Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE) less than or equal to 30 mJ.

constraints.text: Electrical equipment and devices must be intrinsically safe, approved for, or safe for the Class II locations.
Areas where smoking is prohibited must be posted with 'No Smoking' signs.
The facility must have a hot work permit program if hot work is done.
The facility must utilize grounding and bonding, and other methods, for dissipating any electrostatic charge that could be generated.
The facility must select and use powered industrial trucks that are approved for the combustible dust locations.

steps.text: Consult with your Region and HRT before sampling combustible dusts associated with 3D printing operations utilizing metal powders.
Contact your supervisor for direction.

preconditions.text: Planning to sample combustible dusts associated with 3D printing operations utilizing metal powders
CSHOs suspect that the dusts to be sampled may have other hazards

constraints.text: Combustible dust associated with abrasive blasting utilizing steel shot should be considered as a low MIE dust unless determined otherwise.
Combustible dusts associated with 3D printing operations utilizing metal powders can be extremely hazardous.
Self-heating and self-reactive dusts do not have to be dispersed in the air to ignite.
Dangerous-when-wet dusts react with water/moisture to release hydrogen gas. These dusts do not have to be dispersed in air to ignite.

steps.text: Avoid the formation of a dust cloud during the sampling activity.
Utilize bonding and grounding procedures.
Obtain additional information regarding combustible dust MIEs prior to sampling.
Obtain samples necessary to support an enforcement action in a safe manner using procedures in this protocol, and in coordination with their s

preconditions.text: There is a question regarding combustible dust MIEs.
Chemical manufacturer data, employer commissioned MIE testing results, or published data show that the MIE of a material either is ≤ 30 mJ or is likely to be ≤ 30 mJ in the form to be sampled.

constraints.text: Wear PPE including hard hat, safety glasses, and FR clothing.

steps.text: Determine if the sample can be collected from another location or container.
Call the AO and discuss the concerns with your AAD and AD.
Consult with your RO to determine how to proceed.
Issue an imminent hazard notice in accordance with the FOM.
Obtain approval from the AO and RO before collecting samples of low-MIE combustible dusts from uncovered containers or equipment.

preconditions.text: A sample of conductive dust is needed
There are serious concerns about safely sampling dust(s)
The determination after discussion is that it is too dangerous for you to sample
Samples of low-MIE combustible dusts must be collected from containers or equipment that do not fall in the categories covered above

constraints.text: Conductive dusts (whether low-MIE or not) should not be taken from non-conductive containers.
If a sample of a conductive dust is required from a non-metallic or non-conductive container, then HRT assistance is likely necessary.
Use of the HRT for site visits to perform sampling or other activities must be approved by the RO.
LOTO by employer with no confined space entry required.

steps.text: Account for the oxygen deficient atmospheres created by these systems when performing servicing and maintenance.
Determine the maximum pressure in a contained deflagration (Pmax).
Base the design of the system on the worst case scenario properties of the particulate representative within the vessel.
Design and construct enclosures according to the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code.
Design, install, and maintain deflagration pressure containment systems to prevent vessel rupture.

preconditions.text: Employees are required to perform servicing and maintenance on or within these systems.

constraints.text: Oxygen deficient atmospheres are created by these systems.
NFPA provisions include installation of instrumentation/alarm/interlock systems to ensure the desired oxidant concentration reduction.

steps.text: Design, install, and maintain flow-actuated flap valves in accordance with the manufacturer’s recommendations.
Consult NFPA 69 for guidance on flow-actuated flap valves.
Base the system design on the worst case scenario properties of the particulate representative within the vessel at the facility’s process.
Install flow-actuated float valves in ducting connected to a vessel protected by deflagration venting.

preconditions.text: The vessel or dust collector must be protected by deflagration venting.

constraints.text: Do not use flow-actuated flap valves in unvented vessels as their design strengths cannot normally withstand the maximum pressure (Pmax) in a contained deflagration.
Avoid installing flow-actuated flap valves on the exhaust air outlet (downstream) ducting of a dust collector, as the normal direction of air flow may interfere with the flap valve.

steps.text: Refer to NFPA 77 for detailed guidance on static discharge control.
Complete Electrical (Hazard) Class II Considerations.
Verify that electrical equipment in classified locations is intrinsically safe, approved for, or safe for the locations.

preconditions.text: Flammable gases, vapors, and hybrid mixtures are present, or the MIE of the dust cloud is 30 mJ or less (otherwise personnel bonding and grounding is generally not necessary).

constraints.text: Electrical equipment in classified locations must be intrinsically safe, approved for, or safe for the locations.
